title: Dog Frequent Squatting and Straining to Urinate: Triage Guide
meta_desc: Is your dog squatting frequently with little urine? Learn about potential UTIs, urgency levels, and when to seek veterinary care immediately.
content_html:
What Does Frequent Squatting Mean?

When a dog squats repeatedly but only produces small amounts of urine, it usually indicates inflammation or irritation of the lower urinary tract. This sensation, known as pollakiuria, makes the dog feel an urgent need to empty their bladder even when it is nearly empty. The most common causes include urinary tract infections (UTIs), bladder stones, crystals, or in some cases, bladder tumors or prostate issues in male dogs.

Is This an Emergency?

The urgency level for frequent squatting is Medium. While the condition is often painful and requires medical attention, it is typically not life-threatening if the dog is still successfully passing some urine. You should aim to see a veterinarian within 24 hours. However, if your dog is straining and no urine at all is coming out, this is a High urgency emergency (a potential blockage) and requires immediate 24-hour ER care.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Capturing a photo of the urine or a video of the behavior can be a vital tool for your vet. A photo of the urine on a light-colored surface or puppy pad helps the vet assess the color, check for the presence of blood, and see if the urine is cloudy or contains sediment. A video of the squatting behavior helps the veterinarian confirm if the dog is truly straining to urinate (stranguria) or if they are actually trying to defecate, as these two actions can often look very similar to pet owners.

faqs.question: Can I treat my dog's UTI at home with cranberry juice?
faqs.answer: No. While cranberry may have some preventative properties, it cannot cure an active bacterial infection. Your dog will likely need prescription antibiotics or a specific diet to resolve the underlying cause.

faqs.question: How can I tell if my dog is completely blocked?
faqs.answer: If your dog is posturing to go and straining for several minutes without a single drop of urine appearing, or if they are also vomiting and acting very lethargic, you must treat it as a life-threatening emergency.

faqs.question: Will the vet need a urine sample?
faqs.answer: Yes. If possible, try to collect a fresh 'free-catch' sample in a clean container just before your appointment. If you cannot, the vet can perform a procedure called cystocentesis to get a sterile sample directly from the bladder.
